title: practical english usage - Part 115
content:
We   do not normally leave out a/an in negative expressions, after prepositions 4 when     a/an   cannot      be   left   out He's got a friendly face.   It's an extremely hot day.

She's a nice person.    That was a lovely evening.

A/an is common before nouns that are used in descriptions.

A glider is a plane with no engine. Don't use your plate as an ashtray.

She's a doctor.  I'm looking forward to being a grandmother.

what they are, what job they do, or what they are used for.

We   can use a/an when we  classify or define people and things - when we  say c  classifying and defining I would like to live in an old house. (= any old house) A doctor must like people. (= any doctor) We   can use ɑ/ɑn  when we  talk about any one member of a class.

b  any one member of a class.

the;   my,    your,          this,    that,           Section Determiners:       a/an   and           etc;       etc          12 grammar *138   no article with plural and uncountable nouns For expressions like a coffee, a knowledge of Spanish, ►     119.4.  1 19.6.

Everybody likes music. (     ... the music.) not         t Are dogs more intelligent than cats? (     ...  he dogs. .. the cats) (►   140).

But we  usually use no article, not the, to talk about people, things, etc in general Could you pass the salt? (The hearer can see the salt that is wanted.) - We need salt. (= any salt) How are the children? (= the hearer’ s children) - I'm working with children. (The hearer doesn’ t know which  ones.) which  I mean' (►   136). Compare: Instead of no article, we  use the when we  want to say 'You and I both know 4 the For details, ►     139.

We met some nice Danish girls in Scotland. Have you got any matches?

Instead of no article, we  can sometimes use some or any.

3   some      and   any not She speaks very good English. (     .. a very good English.) not We're having terrible weather. (     .. a terrible weather.) And note that we  never use a/an with weather or English.

You've made very good progress. (     .. a very good progress.) not I need information and advice. (     .  .. an information and an advice) English (►   119.3  for a list).

Some nouns that are countable in some other languages are uncountable in 2 confusing       nouns Her coat is made of pure wool.    'What's that?' 'I think it's salt.' Doctors generally work long hours.  He's got very big ears.

There were cats in every room, (     .. a cats.. .) use no article.

with a/an (because a/an has a similar meaning to 'one'). Instead, we  most often Plural and uncountable nouns (e.g. cats, wool ►     137.1) cannot normally be used 1  a/an    not   used unc  ountable       nouns    ____________________ 138  no   article     with    plural     and It's a /eɪ/  reason - it's not the only reason.

I think I'll have a /eɪ/  - chocolate ice cream.

the following word, or when we  want to make a contrast with the.

A is sometimes pronounced /eɪ/  before a hesitation, when we  want to emphasise but not an housewife - the first syllable is stressed.

an hypothesis (a hypothesis is more common) an historic occasion (a historic ... is more common) an hotel (a hotel is more common) is unstressed.

Some people say an, not a, before words beginning with h if the first syllable grammar •  139   the difference between some/any and no article For full details of the uses of some, ►     158; for any, ►     159.

pretty - perhaps six or seven!) would suggest that the speaker is not making clear how  many of them are You've got pretty toes. (A definite number - ten. You've got some pretty toes You've got some great books.

We   do not use some/any when it is clear exactly how  much/many we  are talking the amount.) This heating system doesn't use oil. (The interest is in the type of fuel, not - This heating system hardly uses any oil. (The interest is in the amount.) amount.) Is there water on the moon? (The interest is in the existence of water, not the - Is there any water in the fridge? (The speaker wants a limited amount.) thinking just of the things that need to be bought, not of the amounts.) We need rice, sugar, eggs, butter, beer, and toilet paper. (The speaker is listener wants.) - Would you like some more rice? (An indefinite amount - as much as the (A large number.) There were books on the desk, on the floor, on the chairs,...
